comments.text: One of my favorite country been there once in Okinawa only for a limited stay but I'm still wishing to go back for more, loved the place,the food,the people and the culture.. everything about Japan🙂👍the only downside is that they don't like to learn and speak English that much as they are  very loyal to their national language.Im happy that in my own country we can be able to have anything from Japan to purchase from food,clothing,cosmetics, toys you name it👍

comments.author: @osamu5658
comments.text: 日本人として一言言いたい。体験として着物を着る事は歓迎しますが、着物を着てサングラスをかけることは絶対しない。凄い違和感を感じている。ハッキリ言うとアジア系の女性のサングラスは似合ってない人が多い。カッコ悪いからそもそもサングラスはやめた方がいいですよー！差別という人もいそうだけどファッションセンスの話で人種は関係なく似合ってるか似合ってないか鏡を良く見るべきとの単なる忠告！

replies.author: @osamu5658
replies.text: @聖@聖-e3sな事勿論知ってますよ。むしろ知らない人少ない様な常識じゃ無いですか？貴方は勘違いしてませんか？全外国人ではなく東アジア系を対象にしています。　逆にブルーアイじゃない東アジアの人達が何故サングラス掛けるんですか？白人のサングラスは似合っててカッコいいとも思ってます。　白人の方達でグラサンして着物を着てる人はちょっと見た事無い気がする。伝統に対する敬意と着物に似合わないとのファッションセンスでは？まあファッションは個人の自由だから本人次第で構いません。ただ似合わないとの思いだけです。強制はしてないよファッションセンスとして述べてるだけ。それと皆さんほぼ同じデザインのグラサンなのも不思議でファッションなら色々な好みがあると思うんですが謎です？

replies.author: @聖-e3s
replies.text:
貴方の意見は馬鹿げています。
何故貴方がそれを強制する権利があるのでしょうか？海外の特にブルーアイの方は目が日光に非常に弱い事をご存知ですか？ファッションのためにサングラスをしているのではありません！日本人はサングラスを必要としないだけです。西洋人の方がサングラス姿が多いのはそうゆう事です。

replies.author: @truthseeker9135
replies.text: Either you view Japan with malice, or you fundamentally misunderstand it. Until 1853, when American warships arrived in Japan, flaunted their military might to intimidate the country, and forced it to open its doors, the general Japanese public was just as polite and peaceful as it is today.

comments.author: @travelnearme
comments.text: Thank you for sharing those beautiful pictures of Japan. What I liked most was the balance between ancient tradition and modern life – tranquil temples alongside vibrant neon-lit cities, kindness and discipline shaping daily life. It makes me want to visit Japan in person someday. Has anyone here ever been to Japan? Did it feel as peaceful and respectful as you described?

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: I'm so glad you enjoyed the video and picked up on that unique balance! It truly is a special place. I hope you get to experience it yourself someday.

comments.author: @Henry4KJourneys
comments.text: Watching this makes me want to book a flight to Japan right now!

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: I don't blame you, it truly is a magical place to explore!

comments.author: @ATLAS_UNBOUND_26
comments.text: The contrast between the bustling Tokyo streets and the quiet fishing villages is just breathtaking. This is exactly the kind of Japan I've always wanted to see — real, unfiltered, and beautiful.

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: Thank you so much for that amazing comment! It means a lot to know I was able to show you the real, unfiltered, and beautiful Japan you've always wanted to see.

comments.author: @mejoresviajes6688
comments.text:
Life in Japan always feels both modern and deeply traditional, where quiet streets, ancient customs, and futuristic cities exist side by side.
Would you like to experience daily life in Japan, even for just one day?

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: Thank you for such a thoughtful and insightful comment! You've articulated the beauty of Japan so well. I'm so grateful to be able to share these experiences.

comments.author: @TopUnrealPlanet4K
comments.text: There’s something incredibly powerful about the emotional depth of this video. It doesn’t rush to impress the viewer — it slowly builds curiosity, tension, and fascination in the most natural way possible. That’s what makes it so captivating. The experience lingers in your mind long after it ends. If anyone else feels drawn to that same atmosphere, we recently explored a place on our channel that reveals even more surprising and unsettling secrets.

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: Thank you so much for such a thoughtful and insightful comment! It truly means a lot to hear that the video resonated with you on such a deep level.

comments.author: @LifeNest-3
comments.text: The fact that millions of dollars in lost cash are returned every year says so much about the Japanese social contract. Such a powerful example of mutual respect and Wa.
